_note: Artem particle_sort batch 1 — 15 items at B2, covering Batch 2 verb families (figure/point/rule/work/call/end/take/find/sort/give) plus Batch 1 family extensions (set/bring/come/take/turn). Single-particle blank per item; player taps from 4 chips (1 correct + 3 distractors). Distractors are particles that either pair with the same base_verb under a different meaning (e.g. 'figure out' vs 'figure in') or are tempting via semantic overlap. Themes: business meetings, O&G ops, finance, cycling, Bahrain settings. matched_pattern_id rolled up as base_verb + '_' + correct particle (e.g. 'figure_out') for stats aggregation by PV.

exercises.sentence: We need to figure {1} the root cause before the next planning meeting.
exercises.meaning: figure out — work out the answer to a problem through thinking
exercises.reasoning: 'Figure out' = arrive at the answer through analysis. 'Figure up' isn't standard English. 'Figure in' means 'include as a factor' (you 'factor in' a cost), and 'figure off' isn't a phrasal verb at all.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For solving a problem mentally, 'figure out' is the standard phrasal verb.

exercises.sentence: The auditor pointed {1} two inconsistencies in the cost breakdown.
exercises.meaning: point out — draw attention to something, highlight
exercises.reasoning: 'Point out' = bring something to someone's notice. 'Point to' means 'indicate by gesture' (he pointed to the chart) — physical, not 'highlight in argument'. 'Point up' is rare/literary ('point up the contrast'). 'Point off' isn't standard.
exercises.fallback_feedback: When you draw attention to something in a discussion, you 'point it out'.

exercises.sentence: We can't rule {1} a process leak until the pressure test comes back.
exercises.meaning: rule out — eliminate as a possibility
exercises.reasoning: 'Rule out' = exclude from consideration. 'Rule off' (to rule a line under something) is a literal drawing action. 'Rule down' / 'rule away' aren't established phrasal verbs.
exercises.fallback_feedback: To eliminate a possibility, you 'rule it out'.

exercises.sentence: Finance is still working {1} the exact margin impact of the new tariff.
exercises.reasoning: 'Work out' = arrive at a number or solution. 'Work through' means 'process step-by-step' (work through the logic) — overlaps but emphasises process, not result. 'Work up' = build up gradually (work up an appetite). 'Work over' = beat someone up (slang).
exercises.fallback_feedback: For calculating or arriving at a numeric answer, 'work out' is the standard choice.

exercises.sentence: They called {1} the site visit because of the sandstorm warning.
exercises.meaning: call off — cancel a planned event
exercises.reasoning: 'Call off' = cancel. 'Call out' = name publicly / criticise openly (different meaning). 'Call up' = phone someone or summon for military service. 'Call down' = scold (rare) or invoke (call down a blessing).
exercises.fallback_feedback: To cancel a planned event, you 'call it off'.

exercises.sentence: If we don't lock the spec by Friday, we'll end {1} repeating last quarter's overrun.
exercises.meaning: end up — eventually be in a particular state or situation (often unintended)
exercises.reasoning: 'End up' = land in a final state, often unplanned. 'End in' = finish with (the meeting ended in deadlock) — needs a noun, not a verb. 'End off' = conclude (rarer, formal). 'End out' isn't standard English.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For 'eventually find ourselves in a situation', use 'end up' + verb-ing.

exercises.sentence: Sarah will take {1} the operations role while Hassan is on rotation.
exercises.meaning: take over — assume control or responsibility for something previously held by someone else
exercises.reasoning: 'Take over' = step into a role previously held by someone else. 'Take on' = accept a new responsibility (without it necessarily being someone else's before). 'Take up' = start as a hobby or accept an offer. 'Take in' = absorb information / deceive someone.
exercises.fallback_feedback: When you step into a role previously held by someone else, you 'take it over'.

exercises.sentence: We only found {1} about the regulatory change at the steering committee.
exercises.meaning: find out — discover a fact, learn information
exercises.reasoning: 'Find out' = learn a piece of information. The other particles don't pair with 'find' as a standard phrasal verb in this sense.
exercises.fallback_feedback: When you learn a piece of information, you 'find it out'.

exercises.sentence: Procurement needs to sort {1} the customs paperwork before the rig moves.
exercises.meaning: sort out — resolve, deal with, organise (a problem or admin)
exercises.reasoning: 'Sort out' = resolve or organise. 'Sort through' = examine items one by one to find or organise (sort through emails). Close in meaning but emphasises the act of sifting, not the resolution. 'Sort up' / 'sort off' aren't standard.
exercises.fallback_feedback: When you resolve a logistical mess, you 'sort it out'.

exercises.sentence: After three failed bids, we gave {1} on that contract.
exercises.meaning: give up — stop trying, abandon an effort
exercises.reasoning: 'Give up' = quit, stop trying. 'Give in' = surrender to pressure or temptation (he gave in to her demands) — needs an external pressure being yielded to, not a self-driven 'I'm done'. 'Give out' = run out / stop functioning (the engine gave out). 'Give off' = emit (give off a smell).
exercises.fallback_feedback: To stop trying after sustained effort, you 'give up'.

exercises.sentence: Before the workshop starts, can you set {1} the projector and the whiteboard?
exercises.meaning: set up — prepare or arrange something for use
exercises.reasoning: 'Set up' = arrange or install for use. 'Set out' = begin a journey OR present plans formally (the report sets out the strategy). 'Set off' = begin a trip OR trigger something (set off the alarm). 'Set down' = put down / record formally.
exercises.fallback_feedback: When you arrange or install equipment ready for use, you 'set it up'.

exercises.sentence: Don't bring {1} the supplier dispute in front of the joint-venture partner.
exercises.meaning: bring up — mention or introduce a topic in conversation
exercises.reasoning: 'Bring up' = raise a subject for discussion. 'Bring out' = release (bring out a new model) OR draw out a quality (this brings out his creativity). 'Bring in' = introduce as a new participant (bring in a consultant). 'Bring on' = cause to happen (bring on a headache) or 'let's go' (bring it on).
exercises.fallback_feedback: To mention a topic in conversation, you 'bring it up'.

exercises.sentence: The team came up {1} a workaround that saved the deadline.
exercises.meaning: come up with — devise, think of (an idea or solution)
exercises.reasoning: 'Come up with' = generate or produce (an idea). The third particle matters: 'come up to' = approach physically; 'come up for' = be due for (come up for renewal); 'come up on' = appear suddenly. None mean 'devise'.
exercises.fallback_feedback: When you produce a new idea or solution, you 'come up with it'.

exercises.sentence: I don't think the team has the bandwidth to take {1} another major project this quarter.
exercises.meaning: take on — accept a new responsibility, undertake
exercises.reasoning: 'Take on' = accept a new task or responsibility (not someone else's before — that's 'take over'). 'Take up' = start as a hobby (take up cycling) or accept an offer (take up the offer). 'Take in' = absorb information / shelter someone / deceive. The taken-on/taken-over contrast is the key trap here.
exercises.fallback_feedback: To accept a new responsibility (not previously someone else's), you 'take it on'.

exercises.sentence: We had to turn {1} the bid because their lead time wouldn't fit our schedule.
exercises.meaning: turn down — reject (an offer, a proposal, a candidate)
exercises.reasoning: 'Turn down' = reject or refuse. 'Turn off' = switch off / cause loss of interest. 'Turn back' = reverse direction physically (we turned back at the border). 'Turn away' = refuse entry to a person (the bouncer turned them away) — close to 'reject' but person-focused, not proposal-focused.
exercises.fallback_feedback: To reject an offer or proposal, you 'turn it down'.
